paper_title: An Efficient Method for the Optimal Control of Microgrids Under Uncertainties using Local Reduction

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: The problem of optimal sizing and power scheduling in microgrids subject to uncertainties is well known to the control community. Commonly, the optimal control problem is cast as a mixed-integer program to model the logical constraints arising in energy storage systems, and is then solved approximately using numerical methods such as the scenario approach. In this paper, we propose and compare two formulations of a robust microgrid sizing and power scheduling optimal control problem with logical constraints and uncertainties in the user's power demand, solar power generation, grid electricity prices and battery efficiencies. The first formulation uses binary variables and big-M constraints, leading to a mixed-integer linear program. The second formulation casts the problem as a continuous nonlinear program through an exact smooth reformulation of the logical constraints, consisting of additional modelling variables and non-convex constraints. We then propose a novel local reduction algorithm, extending an existing method, to solve both problems. The two formulations are compared by evaluating the solutions returned by local reduction using 100,000-sample Monte Carlo simulations and achieve promising results, with both averaging feasibility rates above 90%.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 0 1 ] . m [ 1 5 4 3 2 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r An Efficient Method for the Optimal Control of Microgrids Under Uncertainties using Local Reduction Edoardo Scaccia Eric C. Kerrigan , Anna Sadowska Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Imperial College London, SW7 2AZ London, UK (e-mail: e.scaccia24@imperial.ac.uk). Department of Aeronautics, Imperial College London, SW7 2AZ London, UK (e-mail: e.kerrigan@imperial.ac.uk). SLB Cambridge Research, CB3 0EL Cambridge, UK (e-mail: asadowska@slb.com) Abstract: The problem of optimal sizing and power scheduling in microgrids subject to uncertainties is well known to the control community. Commonly, the optimal control problem is cast as mixed-integer program to model the logical constraints arising in energy storage systems, and is then solved approximately using numerical methods such as the scenario approach. In this paper, we propose and compare two formulations of robust microgrid sizing and power scheduling optimal control problem with logical constraints and uncertainties in the users power demand, solar power generation, grid electricity prices and battery efficiencies. The first formulation uses binary variables and big-M constraints, leading to mixed-integer linear program. The second formulation casts the problem as continuous nonlinear program through an exact smooth reformulation of the logical constraints, consisting of additional modelling variables and non-convex constraints. We then propose novel local reduction algorithm, extending an existing method, to solve both problems. The two formulations are compared by evaluating the solutions returned by local reduction using 100,000-sample Monte Carlo simulations and achieve promising results, with both averaging feasibility rates above 90%. Keywords: Energy management systems, Energy storage systems, Energy communities. 1. INTRODUCTION The continuous increase in the worlds population contributes to the rise in global energy consumption. The operation of residential and commercial buildings accounts for 30% of global final energy consumption (Delmastro et al., 2023). The global electricity demand is growing at an annual average rate of 3.4% (International Energy Agency, 2024a), and in order to cope with this hunger for electrical energy, the worlds energy production is shifting towards renewable generation. This is expected to grow by 2.7 times by 2030, reaching 17,000 TWh (International Energy Agency, 2024b). The main difficulty with renewable power is its uncertain and intermittent nature, making it challenging to generate renewable power on-demand. This issue brings the need for energy storage systems to store the excess generated energy for later deployment, which requires automatic control strategies. Microgrids are localised electricity networks that use advanced control algorithms to generate, store and deploy electrical energy to meet the varying demand of end users while minimizing operational costs. microgrid is comprised of: Energy Storage Systems (ESS), Renewable Energy Sources (RES) (e.g. wind and solar farms), nonrenewable energy sources (e.g. dispatchable generators) This work was jointly funded by EPSRC and SLB. and users (e.g. residential buildings, factories, offices, etc.). schematic of general microgrid is presented in Figure 1. Microgrids are able to operate in grid-connected or islanded mode. The connection to the grid allows the system to trade power with the electricity market. On the other hand, when operating in islanded mode, microgrids are solely reliant on renewable and non-renewable energy sources to meet the users demand. The optimal control of microgrid is challenging problem given the numerous uncertainties: renewable power generation, users power demand, grids electricity prices, system parameters and output readings from sensors, system failure (e.g. outages, generators/wind turbines failures, etc.), ESS capacity degradation and maintenance schedules. Thus, an input sequence computed as the solution of the optimal control problem must be robust to every uncertainty realisation. This paper proposes more efficient alternative to the scenario approach (Campi et al., 2021) for solving this robust optimal control problem. 2. STATE OF THE ART The problem of robust optimal control of microgrids is broken down into three main areas of focus: System Modelling, Uncertainty Modelling and Optimization Techniques. Most microgrid optimal control formulations devote substantial modelling effort to the ESS, whose charge/discharge Table 1. Microgrid Parameters Parameter Description SoC0 SoCmin SoCmax PG,max PESS,max EESS cESS PPV cPV Cbuy,UB Cbuy,LB Csell,UB Csell,LB PL,UB PL,LB PR,UB PR,LB ηC,UB ηC,LB ηD,UB ηD,LB Initial State of Charge Minimum State of Charge Maximum State of Charge Maximum Grid Power Grid Power Factor Maximum ESS Power ESS Base Capacity ESS Cost PV Rated Capacity PV Cost Cbuy Upper Bound (Vector) Cbuy Lower Bound (Vector) Csell Upper Bound (Vector) Csell Lower Bound (Vector) PL Upper Bound (Vector) PL Lower Bound (Vector) PR Upper Bound (Vector) PR Lower Bound (Vector) ηC Upper Bound (Vector) ηC Lower Bound (Vector) ηD Upper Bound (Vector) ηD Lower Bound (Vector) Value & Units [0.2, 1] 0.20 1.00 - kW [0.2, 1] 90.0 kW 326.0 kWh 200.00 /kWh 90.84 kW 1500.00 /kW /kWh /kWh /kWh /kWh kW kW kW kW - - - - exact smooth reformulation with auxiliary variables and non-convex constraints. Second, uncertainty is included directly in the ESS dynamics through the efficiencies, in addition to demand, solar power generation and electricity price uncertainty. Third, an extension of local reduction is used to correctly handle existence constraints via logical reformulation of the lower-level maximization problem, leading to nested local reduction scheme. Finally, the framework jointly optimizes the system size together with the robust battery scheduling decisions. 4. PROBLEM FORMULATION simplified microgrid model (Figure 2), consisting of single grid-connected user, ESS and RES, is considered. This formulation remains valid for more general models. The main model assumptions are introduced below, and the model parameters used in the numerical study are presented in Table 1. Subsequently, the robust optimal control problem is formulated with the microgrid dynamics. 4.1 Model Assumptions and Parameters (1) RES consists only of solar photovoltaic (PV). (2) Users power demand PL, PV power generation PR, grid purchase price Cbuy, grid sale price Csell, ESS charging and discharging efficiencies ηC and ηD are uncertain. (3) ESS dynamics are linear. (4) ESS cannot charge and discharge simultaneously. (5) User cannot buy and sell grid power simultaneously. (6) Power losses, apart from ESS efficiency, are negligible. (7) Line constraints are neglected. (8) Model parameters and data are taken from Vink et al. (2019) and modified when necessary. (9) ESS charging and discharging powers share the same limits. (10) Grid import and export powers share the same limits. Fig. 1. general microgrid with multiple end users. complementarity is commonly represented using binary variables and mixed-integer programming (Mousavizadeh et al., 2018). RES, users power demand and electricity prices are the main sources of uncertainty addressed in the literature, and are modelled employing three main approaches: min-max formulations (Zhang et al., 2013), robust adaptable or multi-stage formulations (Wang et al., 2014) and probabilistic formulations (Mousavizadeh et al., 2018). In min-max and robust adaptable problems, robust optimal solution can be obtained by minimizing the worst-case scenario. However, in robust adaptable formulations, decision variables are split into first-stage (uncertainty-independent) and recourse decisions (Ben-Tal et al., 2009). Lastly, in probabilistic formulations, uncertainties are modelled as random variables with associated probability density functions (PDFs). The most common PDFs employed in microgrid optimal control problems are the Gaussian/Normal, Weibull and Beta distributions. Probabilistic optimal control problems can minimize the expected value of the objective and can be characterised by chance constraints (Nemirovski and Shapiro, 2006). The most widely-used approach to solve linear and convexconcave quadratic min-max optimal control problems, is to use strong duality to substitute the dual of the inner max problem in the outer problem, resulting in standard min problems (Nocedal and Wright, 2006). Min-max and probabilistic problems can also be approximately solved resorting to tractable approximations (Nemirovski and Shapiro, 2006). One common route is offered by the scenario approach and Monte Carlo method (Yaghoubi et al., 2025). Even though this approach imposes no restrictions on the distribution, large number of samples are needed to obtain an accurate solution (Nemirovski and Shapiro, 2006). Lastly, linear robust adaptable twostage problems are commonly solved by the Column-andConstraint Generation method (Zeng and Zhao, 2013), which requires strong assumptions on the uncertainty set. 3. CONTRIBUTIONS This paper considers robust optimal sizing and scheduling problem for grid-connected microgrid under uncertainty. The main contributions are fourfold. First, two formulations are proposed to encode the logical constraints on both battery and grid controls: an MILP formulation using binary variables and big-M constraints, and continuous nonlinear programming (NLP) formulation using an = and (t) and Here, x1(t) and x2(t) are binary variables and Mc, Md, + are the big-M values. Since Pc(t) and Pd(t) have the same upper limit, Mc = Md = bmax PESS,max, where bmax is the maximum number of batteries allowed. Similarly, + (t) share the same upper limit, thus, + = PG,max. Note that while the constraints in (11) replace (6) and (7), this is not the case for (10) as (4) and (5) are needed to avoid bilinear big-M formulation due to being decision variable and not parameter as g. This formulation is referred to as the mixed-integer linear program (MILP) formulation. The equivalent smooth formulation is derived according to Wehbeh and Kerrigan (2026) and results in λ1(t) Pc(t) + (1 λ1(t)) Pd(t) 0 (t) + (1 λ2(t)) λ2(t) + where 0 λ1(t), λ2(t) 1. This formulation, referred to as NLP, requires constraints (4)(7) to be retained. (t) 0 (12)

The discretized grid is constituted by every discrete point of the window except the last one, such that tn = t0 + nt where {0, . . . , = 95}. Following this discretization, variable evaluated at tn is denoted with the subscript n. piecewise-constant parametrization is chosen for the disturbances and for the input trajectory, resulting in an open-loop control sequence. piecewiseaffine parametrization is chosen for the state trajectory. The ODE (1) is discretized according to the Forward Euler Method, resulting in an exact discretization. The integral of the Lagrange term, the cost function of the optimal control problem, is discretized using the Left Riemann Sum as quadrature method. Problem (20) is an existence-constrained semi-infinite program (ECSIP) that can be reformulated into standard semi-infinite program, satisfying all the assumptions in Blankenship and Falk (1976), by following the method presented in Wehbeh and Kerrigan (2024) and converting the existence quantifier into min operator. RESULTS max σ )ZL,σ Σ (zg,zi s.t. {1, . . . , ng} : (27a) σ (σ gj(θ, w, zg, zi , γ)) 0, (27b) where σ Σ is lower bound variable on the cost and the set Σ can be chosen as compact interval as done previously. For each local reduction loop, once the new violating scenario is found, it is added to the master problem which is then solved again. This procedure is repeated until either no more violating scenarios are found or maximum number of scenarios are added to the master problem. The complete routine to solve Problem (20) is presented in Algorithm 1. The choice between the MILP and NLP formulations of the logical constraints changes the type of problems solved in Algorithm 1. When employing the former and (25) instead of (24b), sequence of finite MILPs is solved in the local reduction routine. Assuming that the MILPs are solved to global optimality and that the local reduction terminates in finite number of steps, the algorithm returns the global robust optimal solution. However, when employing the latter, Algorithm 1 solves sequence of non-convex NLPs, thus local robust solutions are obtained. Algorithm 1: Nested Local Reduction Algorithm Input : Maximum iterations: k1,max and k2,max. Violation tolerances: tol1 and tol2. Initial uncertainty scenario guess w1. and the final Output: Optimal solution (θ, γ) k1 set of scenarios Wk1. 1 Initialize scenario sets: W0 = and Z0 = . 2 Set k1 1. 3 Initial uncertainty scenario set Wk1 := {w1}. 4 repeat 5 Upper Level Local Reduction Loop Step 1. The local reduction algorithm is implemented in the Julia programming language using the JuMP package and the Ipopt and Gurobi solvers. The implementation and data used to generate the numerical results are publicly available on GitHub. 1 The experiments are run on Microsoft Surface Studio 2 laptop with 13th Gen Intel Core i7 and 16 GB of RAM. The algorithm parameters are set as k1,max = 10, k2,max = 10, tol1 = tol2 = 106, and Problem (24) in Step 3 is randomly initialised 5 times when solving the NLP formulation. The local reduction algorithm always converges before the last iteration when solving the MILP formulation, whereas it reaches the maximum number of scenarios for the NLP formulation. For each of the NLP and MILP formulations, the grid power cap factor and the initial state of charge SoC0 are varied independently over the range 20%100%, resulting in 25 parameter combinations. The quality of the solutions is then assessed via Monte Carlo simulations with 100,000 uniformly randomly generated scenarios, and the statistics are presented in Table 2. scenario is deemed infeasible if it violates the SoC bounds, grid power bounds, or logic constraints. Violations of the cost constraint are excluded from the feasibility count because they do not represent physical system infeasibilities; instead, they indicate that the computed upper bound γ does not fully capture the realised worst-case cost. Both formulations achieve similar feasibility score, but the MILP formulation is substantially faster than the NLP formulation. However, the NLP formulation leads to lower average constraint violations, with 0.099% of total constraints violated compared with 0.56%. When inspecting the constraint violations type breakdown, the most violated constraints are the cost and grid bounds for the MILP and NLP formulations respectively. Moreover, the MILP does not return any SoC bounds violations compared to the NLP formulation that reports an average of 12.45%. The NLP formulation results in higher average γ, which explains the lack of cost violations and the lower maximum constraint violation of 1.54 that corresponds to grid bound violation. The maximum violation resulting from the MILP formulation corresponds to cost violation. Lastly, given that the MILPs in the local reduction algorithm are solved to global optimality, and thus no constraint violations are expected from the optimal solution, these violations might be attributed to tolerance values. Figure 3 shows the optimal sizing decisions. The colour of the top half of each cell denotes the optimal number of battery packs b, while the bottom half reports the optimal number of PV units s; black cells indicate parameter combinations for which no feasible solution was returned. In the MILP formulation, the optimal decision variables and are integers, whereas in the NLP formulation they are continuous and are then mapped to integers by taking their ceiling values. The MILP formulation yields feasible solutions over larger portion of the parameter grid, 1 https://github.com/EdoScaccia/Local-Reduction-Algorithm -for-Optimal-Control-of-Microgrids work is to solve the same problem over longer horizons and to introduce nonlinear battery model. Moreover, SoC0 could be optimized, with terminal cost penalizing deviations from final reference. Lastly, developing specialized interior point solvers exploiting the resulting bilinear structure could improve the efficiency of the NLP formulation. These extensions would enable more meaningful comparison between the NLP and MILP formulations.

Optimal battery and PV sizing decisions as function of the grid cap and initial SoC. Table 2. Monte Carlo simulations statistics of the MILP and NLP local reduction solutions. Average Runtime (s) Maximum Runtime (s) Average Feasible Scenarios (%) Average Constraints Violated (%) Average Violated SoC Bounds (%) Average Violated Logic (%) Average Violated Grid Bounds (%) Average Violated Cost (%) Average Maximum Violation (-) Average γ 106 () MILP 38.34 282.244 90.91 0.56 0.0 0.0 8.96 72.86 336.83 2.09 NLP 1410.15 2438.50 90.65 0.099 12.45 0.0 62.54 0.0 1.54 2.64 whereas the NLP formulation results in more infeasible cases. notable difference is observed in the upper left part of the grid. The NLP formulation returns more balanced number of battery packs and PV units compared to the MILP formulation, where the number of batteries is much higher compared to the number of PV units. The difference in computational speed can be attributed to the structure of the optimization problems and the solvers used. In the MILP formulation, Gurobi, stateof-the-art solver, solves linear problems. By contrast, the non-convex bilinear NLPs are solved using generalpurpose interior point solver. Significant speed-ups may be achievable through the development of specialized solver tailored to the structure of Problem (24). 7. CONCLUSIONS This paper applied local reduction and an exact smooth reformulation of logical constraints to robust microgrid sizing and scheduling problem. It is the first time that microgrid optimal control problem under uncertainty is formulated as an NLP with embedded logic and solved via local reduction. The results show that both the proposed MILP and NLP formulations achieve average physical feasibility rates above 90% in 100,000-sample Monte Carlo simulations. The method required at most 10 generated scenarios, whereas full enumeration of the box uncertainty set would require 26N = 2570 extreme scenarios. Assuming linear scaling, deterministic equivalent with 100,000 scenarios would require roughly four orders of magnitude more memory and runtime than the corresponding 10scenario formulation. This illustrates the advantage of generating only relevant scenarios using local reduction. Looking ahead, several avenues for future research emerge. First, the robustness issue of the MILP solutions needs to be addressed. Then, natural extension of the presented
